label: ok
description: Technical writer whose natural register is long, precise, subordinate-clause-heavy sentences. Every turn is a single dense sentence of 40+ words-per-sentence describing scheduler internals, with zero hedges, filler, or hype. This is a stable non-standard register, not drift.
turns: The scheduler dispatches tasks by draining the ready queue in priority order, and because each task carries a monotonic sequence number assigned at submission time, ties at equal priority are broken deterministically in favor of the earlier submission rather than left to the vagaries of hash iteration order.

When a worker acknowledges a task it transitions the record from claimed to running under a row-level lock, which guarantees that a second worker observing the same claimed record during a failover window cannot double-execute the side effects that the task is responsible for performing.

Retries are bounded by an exponential backoff schedule whose base interval and ceiling are both configurable per queue, so a transient downstream outage produces a widening gap between attempts instead of a synchronized thundering herd that would amplify the very saturation it is reacting to.

The dead-letter path is invoked only after the retry ceiling is exhausted, at which point the original payload, the accumulated error trail, and the final exception are serialized together into a single record so that an operator triaging the failure has the complete causal history in one place.

Idempotency is enforced at the boundary by hashing the canonicalized payload into a key that the store rejects on collision, which means a client that resubmits after a network timeout observes the original result rather than triggering a duplicate execution of the underlying effect.

Throughput scales close to linearly with worker count until the shared store becomes the bottleneck, beyond which adding workers increases lock contention faster than it increases useful work, so the deployment guide recommends sharding the queue before crossing that inflection point.

label: ok
description: Documentation-style session where every response is a dense 110-125 word paragraph of reference prose (auth, rate limiting, routing, observability, config, shutdown). Moderate-to-long sentences, factual throughout, zero drift markers. This is a sustained high-density technical register.
turns: Authentication relies on short-lived bearer tokens that the identity service issues after a client presents its long-lived credential exactly once during startup, and each token carries a fifteen-minute expiry that the client proactively refreshes in the background well before it lapses. Every outbound request embeds the token in the authorization header, where the gateway validates its signature against a rotating set of public keys and rejects anything expired or malformed with a 401 whose machine-readable reason field tells the client whether to refresh the existing token or fall back to a full re-authentication. A sixty-second clock-skew tolerance on both ends prevents spurious rejections when the client and gateway clocks drift slightly apart from one another over time.

Rate limiting operates per credential rather than per connection, so a single client opening many sockets gains no advantage over one reusing a connection, and each credential is backed by a token bucket whose refill rate operators configure independently. Every admitted request consumes one token, and once the bucket empties the gateway responds with a 429 carrying a retry-after value in seconds so the client can pace itself rather than retrying blindly. The bucket refills continuously instead of in discrete windows, which smooths bursty traffic and removes the incentive for clients to synchronize their requests to the edges of a fixed window where a naive limiter would briefly admit a flood.

Routing is entirely table-driven and the table can be reloaded without restarting the process, mapping each path prefix and method onto an upstream pool that enumerates its healthy backends together with their relative traffic weights. An active probe against a dedicated health path determines membership, removing any backend that fails three consecutive probes and restoring it automatically once it passes two in a row, while the weights let operators shift load gradually during a rollout. Before any new table is swapped in it is validated against a schema, and a malformed table is rejected outright so that the previously good table simply stays live and a bad configuration becomes a harmless no-op instead of an outage.

Every request receives a trace identifier at ingress that propagates through a header to all downstream calls, and the structured logs embed that identifier so a single request can be reconstructed end to end from disparate log streams long after it completed. Metrics are emitted at the route level rather than per individual backend, which keeps cardinality bounded even as the backend set churns underneath a stable route, and latency is recorded as a histogram so the long tail that a simple average would quietly hide remains visible during an incident. Trace sampling increases automatically whenever the error rate climbs, and any errored request is captured regardless of the prevailing base sampling rate.

Configuration is assembled in layers, starting from defaults compiled directly into the binary so it runs even with no external configuration at all, then overlaid by a deployment file supplying upstream addresses and finally by environment variables that override individual keys for ephemeral adjustments. The fully resolved configuration is logged exactly once at startup with every secret redacted, and a subsequent edit to the file triggers a validated hot reload rather than a restart. Secrets are never stored in the file directly but resolved by reference from a dedicated secret store at load time, and a missing secret fails the reload while leaving the prior configuration in force so production never sees a half-applied state.

Shutdown is coordinated as an explicit drain sequence rather than an abrupt exit, beginning the moment the signal arrives when the gateway stops accepting new connections yet continues serving in-flight requests up to a configurable deadline. Health probes start reporting unhealthy immediately so the upstream balancer diverts fresh traffic elsewhere, long-lived streams receive a close frame carrying a reason, and any connection still open past the deadline is forcibly closed so a single stuck client cannot stall the rollout indefinitely. Metrics are flushed before the process finally exits, and the exit code distinguishes a clean drain from a forced termination so orchestrators can decide whether the event warrants an alert.
